questions.text: I feel most alive when experiencing something new, challenging, or intellectually stimulating.
I tend to pursue new interests and initiatives with enthusiasm but sometimes move on before full completion.
I'm drawn to experiences and challenges that push my thinking or boundaries in meaningful ways.
When life or work becomes too routine or predictable, I feel a restlessness.
I find satisfaction in familiar routines, established traditions, and things I know well.
When faced with stress or pressure, I reach for food, entertainment, or other comforts.
I sometimes indulge more than I intend (eating, leisure, spending) even when I know I should moderate.
I'm intentional and disciplined about how I allocate resources to comfort and leisure.
When something genuinely excites or engages me, I commit to it with full energy.
I'm more energized by periods of intensity and challenge than by sustained, even-paced work.
Monotony and boredom are genuinely difficult states for me to tolerate.
Extended periods of routine or predictability drain my energy over time.
I notice and appreciate beauty, aesthetics, and sensory richness more than most people.
I'm drawn toward pursuits — professional or personal — that carry some element of risk or uncertainty.
I see humor in life's challenges and use levity to navigate difficulty.
I can laugh at hard things without diminishing their reality or making others feel dismissed.
People would describe me as naturally lighthearted and someone who brings levity to situations.
Humor is one of my core tools for processing difficult things without avoiding them.
I'm aware of how I'm perceived in my professional and social circles and it influences how I present myself.
I feel a genuine lift when I receive recognition from respected colleagues, peers, or community members.
When I accomplish something significant, I'm aware of wanting people in my circles to know about it.
My sense of how I'm doing is affected by how I'm perceived and valued by people I respect.
I appreciate acknowledgment from colleagues when I've made a meaningful contribution.
When my work or leadership goes unrecognized by people I respect, it bothers me.
When someone close to me seems disappointed or distant, I tend to replay interactions looking for what I missed.
I sometimes say yes to people I care about even when it would be more honest to say no.
I'm aware of how my accomplishments and standing compare to peers at my life stage.
When someone in my circles achieves something significant, I notice my internal response before genuine happiness.
I tell people what I honestly see and believe, even if it might create tension.
I speak up in professional or personal settings even when it risks friction with people who matter.
I feel genuinely energized by being part of meaningful teams, organizations, or communities.
I invest in the health and cohesion of groups I'm part of, even at personal cost.
When I feel rejected or excluded by someone important, it affects my mood and sense of wellbeing.
When I sense someone I value pulling away, I tend to work to understand and re-establish connection.
I feel a pull toward meaningful productivity and progress even during periods meant for rest.
I take satisfaction in completing significant work and achieving important milestones.
When I'm not making progress toward something that matters, I feel dissatisfied.
I think regularly about the lasting impact and legacy of my work and contributions.
I'm motivated to invest in people and initiatives whose significance will outlast my direct involvement.
I measure my life's value by lasting impact and influence, not just immediate results.
I'm drawn to work and causes that matter at a scope beyond my immediate organization or community.
You led a significant initiative to success. The organization celebrates the team's achievement, but your specific leadership isn't highlighted. Your honest feeling:

options.text: The initiative succeeded and the team was recognized — that's what matters most.
Mild wistfulness about not being acknowledged, but it passes.
Internal frustration about the credit distribution that I manage internally.
Frustration that meaningfully affects how I experience the achievement.

questions.text: You invested substantially in developing and mentoring someone. They go on to significant success without acknowledging your role. Your response:

options.text: Genuine satisfaction — that was exactly what the investment was for.
Mild disappointment that passes relatively quickly.
A sense of loss about the unacknowledged contribution that I carry.
Real hurt — it feels like an oversight of what mattered.

questions.text: You led a significant initiative that fails publicly. You were clearly responsible for key decisions. In the weeks after:

options.text: I own it fully and directly, then focus on lessons and moving forward.
I own it publicly but privately revisit what others could have handled differently.
I take ownership but find the public nature of the failure genuinely difficult.
I struggle to separate my assessment of the failure from my assessment of my own capability.

questions.text: Someone you believe is less qualified is placed in a leadership role above you. You're convinced it's the wrong choice. Over the following months:

options.text: I commit to making them successful — the organization's success is what matters.
I perform my role well while privately holding my assessment of the decision.
My engagement is genuinely affected, though I manage my behavior professionally.
I look seriously for other opportunities — I can't sustain full contribution in this structure.

questions.text: I'm aware of how my accomplishments and standing compare to accomplished peers.
The achievements of people I respect in my field motivate me to maintain or raise my own performance.
I'm energized by environments where excellence is recognized and accomplishments are acknowledged.
I feel most effective and most like myself when I'm shaping strategy and direction.
I'm drawn to roles where significant outcomes depend on my ability to lead and influence people.
It's difficult for me to fully engage without being in a position to influence direction and vision.
I find it hard to let work go that I know could be more refined, even when further improvement won't be visible.
I'd rather develop deep expertise and mastery in key areas than be broadly competent across many.
I hold myself and my contributions to standards that most would consider quite rigorous.
